tagged_story_for_tts:
Flat surface. Work’s foundation.

The word desk comes from the Latin desca, meaning "table for writing"—a place where thoughts turn into action. Most see it as just furniture, but a desk is a precision tool: its height (standard 29–30 inches) aligns with seated elbow rests to reduce wrist strain; its edges frame focus like a camera’s viewfinder, cutting distractions.

A 16th-century monk’s sloped lectern (the first "desk") held manuscripts at eye level to prevent neck ache during 8-hour copying shifts. Modern desks borrow this ergonomic logic. A standing desk burns 50 more calories per hour than sitting—like walking an extra mile weekly without moving. NASA’s zero-gravity workstations use elastic straps to anchor tools midair, proving desks adapt even where gravity fails.

Three related subjects are ergonomic design, workspaces through history, and how posture shapes thought.
